``` ::: ::: :::
:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

In New Hampshire, sentences have a minimum and a maximum term. However, if a sentence has a maximum of over one year (i.e. a felony sentence), the judge must also impose a 'disciplinary period' of incarceration of 150 days per year of the minimum term that can then be reduced through credit for good conduct. For example, a three to eight year sentence would have a 450 day 'disciplinary period' added on to the minimum term. If a sentence of imprisonment is imposed, the maximum term may not exceed: life for second-degree murder, 15 years for a class A felony, seven years for a class B felony, or one year for a class A misdemeanor. Some crimes carry mandatory minimum sentences. For example, a second or subsequent conviction for the felonious use of a firearm carries a mandatory minimum sentence of three years. A conviction under the state's version of the Armed Career Criminal Act carries a minimum sentence of ten years. Prisoners may be released on parole at the expiration of the minimum terms of their sentences, plus the disciplinary period of incarceration that must be imposed during sentencing, and minus any credit for good conduct. So, for example, if an individual is given a sentence of 10 to 25 years, they must serve their 10 year minimum and, in addition, 1,500 days (or about four years, one month, and 10 days) of a disciplinary period. However, through good time credit, the inmate can pare the 14 years plus that must be served before parole release eligibility back down to 10 years. Thus, the disciplinary period and good time are designed to offset each other.


People who were Eligible for Parole

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 87% (481 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.



In 2020, there were 21 people who were incarcerated for a parole revocation and were eligible for parole but not yet released from prison.

``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} :::
## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population

In 2020, there were 565 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 25% of the parole-eligible prison population.




``` ::: :::

## Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 218 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 39% of the parole-eligible prison population.
